caption:
The wall between your bank and your wallet was always somebody's business model.

Send a wire to your Infinex account. It lands as USDC on-chain.

One account. Dollars in, euros in, pesos in. The wall isn't there anymore.

The wire used to leave the chain. Days of routing, intermediaries, an off-ramp pretending to be a bridge.

Your Infinex account now takes ACH, SEPA, and wire directly. Fiat lands as USDC in your wallet.

The bank rail ends where the wallet begins. Same motion, one surface.

The wire still clears in two days. The account number still belongs to a bank you've never walked into.

Now that account number is yours inside Infinex. ACH, SEPA, wire — the deposit lands as USDC in your wallet.

Six currencies in. One balance out. The rail you came from no longer decides what you hold.

The wire, the routing number, the three-day wait. That was the interface.

Infinex account numbers now accept ACH, SEPA, and wire. Deposits land on-chain as USDC.

USD, EUR, GBP, MXN, BRL, COP — six currencies, one wallet, no seam.

The wire, the ACH transfer, the SEPA rail. Old machinery, still humming.

Send from your bank to your Infinex account number. It lands as USDC in your wallet.

One deposit. No crossing required.

Every chain stood between a user and MegaETH. Bridge, gas, swap, three transactions, three places to lose the thread.

Swidge takes MegaETH from any supported chain in one transaction. Bridge, gas, destination DEX, handled.

The chain stops being the thing you cross. It's just where you land.

MegaETH is the fastest chain. Speed there has been gated by the work of getting capital in.

Swidge bridges in from any supported source. One transaction covers the bridge, the gas, the destination swap.

The route is open.

Every bridge has been a route to plan. A chain to add. A gas token to find. A DEX to open after.

Swidge now reaches MegaETH from any supported chain. Bridge, gas, destination swap — one transaction.

You don't route in. You arrive.

The chain you wanted to reach was always somewhere else.

MegaETH is now a Swidge destination. One transaction from any supported source — bridge, gas, and the destination DEX, handled.

You arrive already moving.

MegaETH was a destination you had to plan for.

Swidge bridges in from any supported source. One transaction carries the gas and the DEX hop with it.

You decide. You're already there.

Choosing between Aave, Morpho, Pendle, Maple was never the interesting part of the work.

The vaults are live. An agent routes the capital and rotates when the math changes.

Every rebalance opens its reasoning to you. Read it, or don't.

Autonomous yield has always asked for trust without offering proof.

The vaults are live. An agent routes across Aave, Morpho, Pendle, Maple.

Every rebalance shows its reasoning. The machine acts, then explains.

Capital used to sit where you put it.

Yield vaults are live. An agent routes across Aave, Morpho, Pendle, Maple.

Every rebalance carries its reasoning. Read it, weigh it, decide what you make of it.

Allocation across Aave, Morpho, Pendle, Maple has always been a judgment call.

The vault now makes that call, and shows its work on every rebalance.

Reasoning, visible. Yield, live.

Delegation has always asked you to trust something you couldn't see.

The vaults are live. An agent rotates across Aave, Morpho, Pendle, Maple.

Every rebalance opens its reasoning to you.

Two accounts. Two passkeys. Two portfolios to reconcile every morning.

That's over. Hyperliquid spot trades natively from your Infinex account.

One key. The book opens where you already are.

The orderbook used to live somewhere else. Your account followed it there, or it didn't move.

Hyperliquid spot now trades inside Infinex. One account, one passkey, both sides.

The liquidity came to where you already are.

The choice between venues was always a tax on the user.

Spot Hyperliquid trades natively inside Infinex. One account, one passkey, both sides.

The wall was never load-bearing.

Two accounts. Two logins. Two halves of the same portfolio.

Hyperliquid spot now trades natively inside Infinex. One account, one passkey.

The wall was never the point. The market was.

The wall between two accounts was always arbitrary.

Spot Hyperliquid trades inside Infinex now. One account, one passkey, both sides.

The seam is gone. What's left is the trade.

The seed phrase was always the toll booth. Twelve words between a person and their money, and one bad afternoon away from losing it all.

Infinex accounts sign in by passkey. Same account, any device, no phrase to write down or lose.

The door was heavier than the room behind it. We took the door off.

The seed phrase was the toll booth crypto never removed.

Infinex accounts authenticate by passkey. The account travels with you, device to device.

No paper, no metal plate, no envelope in a drawer. Just the account, where you are.

The seed phrase was always a burden disguised as security.

Infinex accounts authenticate with a passkey.

Your account, reachable from any device you sign in on.

The seed phrase was always a liability dressed as security. One slip, one fire, one forgotten drawer — and the account was gone.

Infinex accounts now authenticate by passkey.

The account travels with you, device to device, nothing to write down, nothing to lose.

The seed phrase was always the price of entry.

Now the account is a passkey. Sign in, and it follows you across devices.

Nothing to write down. Nothing to lose. Just the door, opening when you do.

Sunday, 02:00 UTC. The perps engine goes quiet for about ten minutes.

Open positions hold where they are. New orders queue and release when the engine returns.

A short stillness, then the rails carry through.

Sunday, 02:00 UTC. The perps engine goes down for roughly ten minutes.

Open positions stay as they are. New orders queue and clear when the window closes.

Quiet work, done in the quiet hour.

Sunday 02:00 UTC, the perps engine goes down for about ten minutes.

Open positions hold. New orders queue through the window.

A short pause, then back to the book.

Sunday, 02:00 UTC. The perps engine goes down for service.

Ten minutes, give or take. Open positions sit where you left them; new orders queue and clear when the lights come back.

A quiet window, scheduled, and then the book breathes again.

Sunday, 02:00 UTC. The perps engine goes quiet for about ten minutes.

Open positions hold where they are. New orders queue and clear when the engine wakes.

A short window, then back to flow.

Private Send is no longer in beta.

It's available to every user, starting today.

Send a payment. Your wallet history stays your own.

Private Send spent its time in beta quietly. Sending money never required a public ledger of your history.

Today it leaves beta. Every user has it.

Pay someone. The transfer carries no past with it.

A wallet has always carried its history in the open. Every payment, a paper trail anyone could read.

Private Send is now generally available. Send without your chain history walking in the door first.

The past stops introducing you.

A payment used to drag your whole history behind it. Every counterparty inherited your ledger.

Private Send is GA. Send without handing over the trail.

The default quietly moves.

Privacy was never meant to live in beta.

Private Send is GA. Every user, every payment.

Send without dragging your history behind you.

Ten million accounts.

No seed phrases. Yield that runs itself.

The wall was never the chain. It was everything we asked people to carry across it.

Ten million accounts.

The barrier was never the user. It was the door.

Passkeys opened it. The vaults did the rest.

Ten million accounts.

The friction was the product. Remove it and this is what's underneath.

Passkeys, vaults, the rest of what's coming.

Ten million accounts.

A passkey, a vault, no seed phrase between them.

This is what the door looks like once you stop guarding it.

Ten million accounts.

The gate was never the chain. It was the seed phrase, the signature popup, the bridge tab open in the background.

A passkey opens the door. The vault keeps working while you sleep.

The vault doesn't decide what risk you carry.

You set the ceiling, the allowed protocols, the rebalance cadence. The agent works inside those walls.

Every rebalance comes back as a report against the constraints you locked.

Delegation without governance is just hope wearing a uniform.

Set the vault's limits yourself: max risk, allowed protocols, rebalance cadence.

Each rebalance returns to you, measured against the lines you drew.

The agent acted. You watched.

Now you set the bounds. Max risk, which protocols, how often it rebalances.

It reports back against the lines you drew.

Delegation without boundaries isn't delegation. It's abdication.

So the vault answers to you: max risk, allowed protocols, rebalance cadence. You set them. They hold.

Every rebalance comes back against the line you drew.

Delegation without limits isn't delegation. It's abdication.

Set the agent's ceiling: max risk, the protocols it may touch, how often it rebalances.

Each rebalance comes back as a report against the bounds you drew.

The old yield UI is gone.

One vault now. One position. The agent runs it.

You stopped hunting yield. You didn't notice when.

The old yield aggregator is gone.

One vault now. One funded position. The agent reads the field.

You hold the want. The work routes itself.

The old yield screen is gone. It asked you to compare, weigh, decide, repeat.

In its place: a single vault. One funded position, one agent doing the reading.

You stopped being the analyst. You became the one the work is done for.

The old yield aggregator is gone.

One vault now holds the position. One interface, one place to look.

The comparing, the rebalancing, the tab-switching — lifted off the desk.

The old yield aggregator is gone.

One vault now holds the position. One interface, one funded line.

The weight settles where it belongs, and the surface goes quiet.
